title: Transition Defense: Stop the Ball, Find Shooters

lesson.title: Sprint back, build the wall
lesson.body_md:
## Transition is where games are won and lost

Your offense just turned the ball over, missed a shot, or had a kickout slip. The other team is racing the other way. The next 4 seconds decide whether they get a layup or a contested jump shot.

Most players walk back. Don't. **Sprint.**

## The 2-stage rule

### Stage 1: Stop the ball

The **first** player back doesn't go find their man. They go find the **basketball**.

- Get in front of the ball handler.
- Force them to slow down or change direction.
- Don't reach. Don't gamble. Just be a wall they have to read.

This buys your teammates 1–2 extra steps to recover. That's the entire game.

### Stage 2: Find shooters

The **second and third** players back fan out to the **wings and corners**, not the rim.

Why? Because in transition, the dangerous shots are:
1. The trailing three (a guard pulling up early)
2. The corner three (a wing sprinting wide)

Everyone wants to protect the rim. The rim is already protected — your big is back. The threes are what kill you.

## The 3 sprint rules

1. **Sprint to the level of the ball.** Don't stop short. Don't sprint past it.
2. **Eyes over your shoulder.** As you run back, count their players. Outnumbered? Buy time. Even? Get matched up.
3. **Find a body, not your man.** First defender takes the closest threat, not their original assignment. Sort it out at the next dead ball.

## When to take the charge

If the ball handler is bearing down on the rim and you're set with two feet planted **outside the restricted area**, take it. A charge in transition is a momentum killer for the offense.

Don't take it if:
- You're moving sideways (it'll be a block)
- You're inside the restricted arc (no charge call)
- A teammate is already there (don't double up — go find a shooter)

## The 3 mistakes that cost games

1. **Watching the shot.** The miss happens, you stand there, the offense leaks out for a layup.
2. **Backpedaling.** You give up ground every step. Turn and sprint, then square up.
3. **All running to the same spot.** Five guys in the paint = three open shooters.

## Communication

- **"Back!"** when a shot goes up — everyone sprints.
- **"I got ball!"** from the first player back.
- **"I got trailer!"** from the player picking up the late shooter.

Loud, early, and constant.

## What you're training

The scenarios put you in moments where the ball just changed hands. You have to choose: stop the ball, find the trailer, or sprint to a shooter. The wrong choice gives up an easy 2 or an easy 3. The right choice turns their fast break into a stalled half-court possession.

## Coach's cue

> *"Sprint, sort, swarm. Stop the ball first. Find shooters second. Match up last."*

Transition defense is effort plus brain. The teams that get back together win games.
